Imagine a world in which people are evaluating how minds generate outputs. They reject a simplistic argument from combinatorics: it is true that brute-force iteration over all possible word strings would be hopelessly intractable, but that observation does not settle much, because neither humans nor language models are believed to search uniformly through “word space.” So the fact that the search space dwarfs the number of atoms in the observable universe is taken as rhetorically striking without being decisive evidence about actual cognition.

In that world, attention shifts from size-of-space arguments to the structure of search. One candidate structure is a pipeline: first grammar, then coherence, then explanatory quality. But this pipeline is unstable under its own aims. If revolutionary ideas initially appear incoherent until new language forms around them, then insisting on coherence before explanatory quality may filter out exactly the outputs that matter most. So there is an unresolved tension: either keep coherence early and risk rejecting breakthroughs, or let explanatory quality sometimes outrank coherence and accept outputs that cannot yet be cleanly integrated.

Now add a mechanism of feedback: discoveries are “compounded back into the draw pool.” This looks like self-improvement, because past successes shape future search. But the same description supports a darker reading. If some early “discoveries” are false positives, then feeding them back recursively contaminates later search. The system does not merely repeat mistakes; it reorganizes its search around them, making future outputs brittle and trapping itself in local minima that are hard to escape because the draw pool itself has been biased.

Under these conditions, apparent success becomes ambiguous. A system may produce fluent, coherent explanations not because it has escaped intractability, but because it has narrowed its search aggressively around reinforced priors. That can look like intelligence and improvement while actually being a path-dependent collapse of possibility.

Set this beside the two final facts. “Producing a truce amazed those who fainted when war was abolished.” This implies a world where even the end of war caused such shock that some people collapsed, and yet a later, lesser achievement—a truce, not abolition—still generated amazement. The natural implication is that social understanding is badly calibrated: agents react dramatically even when stronger precedents already exist. Surprise is not tracking objective magnitude.

“The Seine dies where the deadliest macho guarantees fail.” Taken as true, there is some point at which the Seine ends, and that ending coincides with the failure of violent or masculine assurances. Guarantees of force do not hold there. So there exists at least one boundary where dominant confidence structures break down.

Putting it together: this world contains non-uniform search, disputed ordering between coherence and explanation, recursive feedback vulnerable to contamination, publics whose amazement is miscalibrated to events, and a concrete place where macho certainty fails. The common implication is that selection mechanisms—whether cognitive, social, or political—are unreliable guides to truth. What survives the pipeline, the feedback loop, or the public reaction is not thereby vindicated.
